title: Exiting Buy-to-Let in Greenwich: A Landlord's Guide to Selling Rental Property
excerpt: A comprehensive guide for Greenwich landlords looking to sell buy-to-let property, covering tax, tenants, regulations, and the fastest exit strategies.
metaDescription: Guide for Greenwich landlords exiting buy-to-let. Selling with tenants, tax planning, regulatory changes, and how to sell rental property fast in the Royal Borough.
content:
Why Greenwich Landlords Are Selling Up

The buy-to-let landscape in the Royal Borough of Greenwich has shifted dramatically. What was once a reliable investment — purchase a flat in Woolwich, a terrace in Charlton, or a house in Plumstead, let it out, and enjoy capital growth — has become a complex, expensive, and increasingly regulated undertaking. Growing numbers of Greenwich landlords are concluding that the numbers no longer work and it's time to exit.

This guide examines the forces driving the landlord exodus, the practical challenges of selling rental property, and the options for achieving a quick, efficient exit from buy-to-let in Greenwich.

The Pressures on Greenwich Landlords

Mortgage Cost Increases

The sharp rise in buy-to-let mortgage rates has been the most immediate trigger for landlord exits. A Greenwich landlord who fixed at 2% in 2021 might now face rates of 5-6% on renewal. On a typical rental flat in Woolwich with a £250,000 mortgage, this could add £500-£800 per month. Even with Greenwich's strong rental demand, most landlords cannot pass these increases fully to tenants, squeezing margins to unsustainable levels.

Tax Changes

The removal of mortgage interest relief — fully phased in since April 2020 — fundamentally changed the economics of buy-to-let for higher-rate taxpayers. Many Greenwich landlords now pay tax on rental income that's entirely consumed by mortgage interest. The 3% stamp duty surcharge on additional properties added further friction to portfolio expansion.

Regulatory Tightening

The Renters' Reform Bill, with its proposed abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions, strengthened tenant rights, and new landlord registration requirements, adds uncertainty and operational complexity. The Royal Borough of Greenwich has its own additional licensing requirements for certain HMOs and rental properties. For many part-time landlords who entered the market for passive income, the regulatory burden has become anything but passive.

Energy Efficiency Requirements

Future regulations are expected to require rental properties to achieve a minimum EPC rating of C by 2028. For landlords with older Greenwich properties — Victorian terraces in Charlton, Edwardian houses in Blackheath, or pre-war homes in Eltham — achieving this standard could require substantial investment in insulation, windows, heating systems, and other upgrades.

Challenges of Selling Tenanted Property in Greenwich

Limited Buyer Pool

Most residential property buyers are owner-occupiers who want to move into their purchase immediately. A property with an existing tenant dramatically narrows the buyer pool to investors — and many investors are currently selling rather than buying. In Greenwich's market, this can mean significantly longer marketing periods and lower offers.

Presentation and Access

Tenant cooperation with viewings cannot be guaranteed. Properties may not be presented to their best advantage, with personal belongings, wear and tear, and tenant lifestyle choices all potentially impacting buyer impressions. In areas like Eltham and Charlton, where the market is competitive, a poorly presented property can languish while comparable vacant homes sell quickly.

Ending Tenancies

Obtaining vacant possession requires following the correct legal procedures. Section 21 notices currently require two months' notice (subject to compliance with all legal prerequisites), and if the tenant doesn't leave voluntarily, court proceedings can add considerable time. With Section 21 abolition on the horizon, future landlords may need to rely on specific grounds for possession, which could make vacant possession harder to obtain.

Tax Considerations

Capital Gains Tax

Selling a buy-to-let triggers Capital Gains Tax on any profit. The gain is the sale price minus the purchase price (plus allowable costs). CGT rates on residential property are 18% (basic-rate) and 24% (higher-rate), with a much-reduced annual exempt amount.

For Greenwich landlords who purchased years ago, gains can be substantial. A flat in Woolwich bought for £180,000 in 2012 and now worth £350,000 generates a taxable gain of potentially £140,000 after costs. At 24%, that's a CGT bill approaching £34,000. Professional tax planning before selling is essential.

Timing Strategies

Structuring the sale within a tax year where your income is lower can reduce the CGT rate from 24% to 18%. If selling multiple properties, spreading sales across tax years maximises use of annual exempt amounts. These strategies require planning, so consulting a tax adviser well before selling is recommended.

Exit Options for Greenwich Landlords

Traditional Sale with Vacant Possession

Ending the tenancy, preparing the property, and selling through an estate agent achieves the highest price but involves the longest timeline: 2+ months to end tenancy, 1-2 months for vacancy and preparation, 4-6 months for the sales process. Total: 8-12 months, during which you have no rental income but continued costs.

Sale to Another Investor

Selling with the tenant in place to another investor avoids the vacancy process but limits your buyer pool and typically reduces the price. Investor buyers calculate offers based on rental yield, not vacant possession value, and in the current climate, they're looking for discounts. In Greenwich, investor demand is strongest for properties in regeneration areas like Woolwich with strong rental yields and growth potential.

Cash Property Buyer

Cash buyers like Sell My House Fast Greenwich can purchase your rental property regardless of tenancy status. We buy with tenants in situ — the tenancy transfers to us as new owners, with all tenant rights protected — or we can coordinate with you on the vacancy process before completion. The entire transaction can complete in 7-21 days, with all legal fees covered. For landlords who want out quickly and cleanly, this offers the fastest and most certain exit.

Practical Exit Planning

If you're considering selling your Greenwich rental property, advance planning can save significant sums:

  • Review your mortgage: Check early repayment charges and any exit fees
  • Get specialist tax advice: A buy-to-let tax specialist can help structure the sale to minimise CGT
  • Understand your tenancy: Review the tenancy agreement, deposit protection, and compliance with all legal requirements — deficiencies can delay or complicate a sale
  • Gather documentation: Compile purchase receipts, improvement invoices, Gas Safety Certificates, EPC, and deposit protection evidence
  • Consider your tenant: If you have a good tenant, they may want to purchase the property themselves — this can sometimes be the simplest route

Portfolio Sales

Landlords with multiple Greenwich properties may want to sell their entire portfolio simultaneously. Cash buyers can often accommodate portfolio purchases, providing a single transaction for multiple properties and a coordinated timeline. This simplifies the legal and tax process and provides a clean, complete exit from buy-to-let.

Getting Started
